---
jupyter: ames-venv
---

```{python}
# PART 1: UNDERSTAND & CLEAN THE DATA
# PART 1: A1 - Load & Inspect 
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_excel("../data/AmesHousing-2.xls")

df.head()
```

```{python}
#Shape check confirms 2,930 rows × 82 columns — matches the dataset's known size exactly, so the load is correct.
df.shape
```

```{python}
# how many columns are numbers vs. text 
print("Numeric columns:", df.select_dtypes(include="number").shape[1])

print("Categorical columns:", df.select_dtypes(include="object").shape[1])

df.dtypes.value_counts()
```

```{python}
#which columns have missing values, worst first
# 27 columns have missing values. 
#The top offenders (Pool QC, Misc Feature, Alley, Fence) are not really “missing” in the broken sense
#they are NaN because the house simply doesn't have that feature (no pool, no alley access, etc.).
#This distinction drives later the cleaning strategy 
missing = df.isna().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)

missing = missing[missing > 0]

print(f"{len(missing)} columns have missing values")

missing.head(15)
```

```{python}
# PART 1: A2 - EDA — Distributions, Target Analysis, Correlation, Feature–Target Relationships
# Target analysis (analyzing the variable you are trying to predict) - SalePrice summary 
df["SalePrice"].describe()
```

```{python}
#EDA look at the shape of SalePrice (Distribution: SalePrice histogram)
# SalePrice is right-skewed, piling up around $130k–200k with a long tail to $700k+. 
# This happens because price has a hard floor near $0 but no real ceiling. 

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

sns.histplot(df["SalePrice"], kde=True)

plt.title("SalePrice Distribution")

plt.xlabel("SalePrice")

plt.ylabel("Count")

plt.show()
```

```{python}
#Correlation study
correlation = df.corr(numeric_only=True)["SalePrice"].sort_values(ascending=False)

correlation.head(10)
```

```{python}
premium_threshold = df["SalePrice"].quantile(0.75)
print("Premium threshold:", premium_threshold)
```

```{python}
# EDA - Overall Qual vs SalePrice (Feature-target relationships)
# Overall Qual (0.80) and Gr Liv Area (0.71) are by far the strongest single predictors of price, 
# followed by garage size and basement size. 
# This ranking directly shaped which engineered features were prioritized in Part B 
# (e.g. an Overall Qual × size interaction).

plt.figure(figsize=(9,5))
sns.boxplot(x="Overall Qual", y="SalePrice", data=df)
plt.title("SalePrice by Overall Quality")
plt.xlabel("Overall Qual (1=worst, 10=best)")
plt.ylabel("SalePrice")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Eda, step 3 out of 3 Heatmap next — visual version of the correlation table
plt.figure(figsize=(12,9))
top_corr_cols = correlation.head(15).index  # top 15 features most correlated with SalePrice
sns.heatmap(df[top_corr_cols].corr(), annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="coolwarm")
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap — Top Features vs SalePrice")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#EDA visualization  a scatter plot of Gr Liv Area vs SalePrice. 
# :  Three points stand out clearly: homes with 4,600–5,600 sq ft of living area that sold for under $200,000 
# far below what their size predicts. All three also have Overall Qual = 10, later removed. 
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
plt.scatter(df["Gr Liv Area"], df["SalePrice"], alpha=0.4)
plt.xlabel("Gr Liv Area (sq ft)")
plt.ylabel("SalePrice")
plt.title("SalePrice vs Living Area")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#PART 3: A3 - Clean - missing values
none_means_missing = [
    "Pool QC", "Misc Feature", "Alley", "Fence", "Fireplace Qu",
    "Garage Type", "Garage Finish", "Garage Qual", "Garage Cond",
    "Bsmt Qual", "Bsmt Cond", "Bsmt Exposure", "BsmtFin Type 1", "BsmtFin Type 2",
    "Mas Vnr Type"
]

for col in none_means_missing:
    df[col] = df[col].fillna("None")

print("Remaining missing values:")
df.isna().sum()[df.isna().sum() > 0].sort_values(ascending=False)
```

```{python}
# Missing values 
#If a house has no garage/basement, these should be 0. 
zero_means_missing = [
    "Garage Yr Blt", "Mas Vnr Area", "Bsmt Full Bath", "Bsmt Half Bath",
    "BsmtFin SF 1", "BsmtFin SF 2", "Bsmt Unf SF", "Total Bsmt SF",
    "Garage Cars", "Garage Area"
]
for col in zero_means_missing:
    df[col] = df[col].fillna(0)

print("Remaining missing values:")
df.isna().sum()[df.isna().sum() > 0].sort_values(ascending=False)
# Lot Frontage (490 missing) and Electrical (1 missing) are genuinely unrecorded values, not “doesn't exist” cases. 
# Filling them with a median/most-common value calculated now would leak information from what will become the test set. 
# These two are filled later, inside the leakage-safe preprocessing Pipeline, 
# using statistics computed from the training split only (SimpleImputer with strategy=“median” / “most_frequent”).
```

```{python}
# Detect and treat outliers
outliers = df[(df["Gr Liv Area"] > 4000) & (df["SalePrice"] < 300000)]
print(f"Found {len(outliers)} outlier rows:")
outliers[["Gr Liv Area", "SalePrice", "Overall Qual"]]

#The same 3 anomalous sales spotted visually in the Gr Liv Area scatter plot,
# now formally identified and removed using a documented rule (Gr Liv Area > 4,000 sq ft and SalePrice < $300,000).
```

```{python}
# Removing outliers (overall quality too high)
df = df.drop(outliers.index).reset_index(drop=True)
print("New shape after removing outliers:", df.shape)
```

```{python}
print(df.shape)
df.columns.tolist()
```

```{python}
# PART 2: ENGINEER & SELECT FEATURES
# PART 2: B1 - Creating engineered features 

# Total finished square footage — combines basement + both floors
df["TotalSF"] = df["Total Bsmt SF"] + df["1st Flr SF"] + df["2nd Flr SF"]

# Age of the house at time of sale (domain logic)
df["HouseAge"] = df["Yr Sold"] - df["Year Built"]

# Years since last remodel at time of sale
df["RemodAge"] = df["Yr Sold"] - df["Year Remod/Add"]

# Total bathrooms, weighting half-baths as 0.5 (standard real-estate convention)
df["TotalBath"] = (
    df["Full Bath"] + 0.5*df["Half Bath"] +
    df["Bsmt Full Bath"] + 0.5*df["Bsmt Half Bath"]
)

# Interaction: quality × size — captures that a big AND nice house is worth more than either alone
df["Qual_x_SF"] = df["Overall Qual"] * df["TotalSF"]

print("New shape:", df.shape)
df[["TotalSF", "HouseAge", "RemodAge", "TotalBath", "Qual_x_SF"]].describe()
```

```{python}
# Investigated rather than ignored: exactly 1 row (built 2007, sold 2007, “remodeled” 2008) had a logically impossible negative RemodAge.
# Clipped at 0 rather than dropping the row,
# since every other field for that house was valid — a minimal, defensible fix.

df["RemodAge"] = df["RemodAge"].clip(lower=0)
print("Min RemodAge now:", df["RemodAge"].min())
```

```{python}
df[["TotalSF", "HouseAge", "RemodAge", "TotalBath", "Qual_x_SF"]].describe()
```

```{python}
# Age boundaries 
df["AgeCategory"] = pd.cut(
    df["HouseAge"],
    bins=[-1, 10, 30, 60, 200],
    labels=["New (0-10y)", "Recent (10-30y)", "Established (30-60y)", "Old (60y+)"]
)

print(df["AgeCategory"].value_counts())
```

```{python}
#Adding additional features (outdoor space and amenity flags)
df["TotalPorchSF"] = (
    df["Open Porch SF"] + df["Enclosed Porch"] +
    df["3Ssn Porch"] + df["Screen Porch"] + df["Wood Deck SF"]
)

df["HasPool"] = (df["Pool Area"] > 0).astype(int)
df["HasGarage"] = (df["Garage Area"] > 0).astype(int)
df["HasFireplace"] = (df["Fireplaces"] > 0).astype(int)
df["Has2ndFloor"] = (df["2nd Flr SF"] > 0).astype(int)

print(df[["TotalPorchSF","HasPool","HasGarage","HasFireplace","Has2ndFloor"]].describe())
```

```{python}
# PART C - SPLIT & VALIDATE PROPELY, and applying one feature selection (Lasso INSIDE a Pipeline)
# Part C - C1 - Train & split
# 80/20 split, stratified by SalePrice quartile bins rather than premium_home directly 

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

RANDOM_STATE = 42

# Bin SalePrice into quartiles, purely to balance the split — not used as a feature or target
price_bins = pd.qcut(df["SalePrice"], q=4, labels=False)

train_df, test_df = train_test_split(
    df, test_size=0.2, random_state=RANDOM_STATE, stratify=price_bins
)

print("Train shape:", train_df.shape)
print("Test shape:", test_df.shape)
```

```{python}
# C2 - Deriving premium_home (leakage-safe)
# Compute the threshold using ONLY the training rows
premium_threshold = train_df["SalePrice"].quantile(0.75)
print("Premium threshold (from train only):", premium_threshold)

# Apply that exact same number to both sets
train_df = train_df.copy()
test_df = test_df.copy()
train_df["premium_home"] = (train_df["SalePrice"] > premium_threshold).astype(int)
test_df["premium_home"] = (test_df["SalePrice"] > premium_threshold).astype(int)

print("\nTrain premium_home distribution:")
print(train_df["premium_home"].value_counts(normalize=True).round(3))
print("\nTest premium_home distribution:")
print(test_df["premium_home"].value_counts(normalize=True).round(3))
```

```{python}
# C3 - Building X/Y and the preprocessing pipeline
#  Separate features (X) from targets (y)
# Drop targets and ID-like columns from the feature set
drop_cols = ["SalePrice", "premium_home", "Order", "PID"]

X_train = train_df.drop(columns=drop_cols)
X_test = test_df.drop(columns=drop_cols)

# Regression target
y_train_reg = train_df["SalePrice"]
y_test_reg = test_df["SalePrice"]

# Classification target
y_train_clf = train_df["premium_home"]
y_test_clf = test_df["premium_home"]

print("X_train shape:", X_train.shape)
print("X_test shape:", X_test.shape) 
```

```{python}
#2  Tell the Pipeline which columns are numeric vs. categorical
numeric_cols = X_train.select_dtypes(include="number").columns.tolist()
categorical_cols = X_train.select_dtypes(exclude="number").columns.tolist()

print(f"{len(numeric_cols)} numeric columns")
print(f"{len(categorical_cols)} categorical columns")
print("\nCategorical columns:", categorical_cols)
```

```{python}
# MS SubClass is stored as a number but is actually a category code, not a true numeric scale
numeric_cols.remove("MS SubClass")
categorical_cols.append("MS SubClass")

print(f"{len(numeric_cols)} numeric columns")
print(f"{len(categorical_cols)} categorical columns")
```

```{python}
# Leakage-safe preprocessing Pipeline -- fit ONLY on X_train, never on the full df
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.preprocessing import OneHotEncoder, StandardScaler

# Numeric pipeline: fill missing values with median, then scale
numeric_transformer = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
    ("scaler", StandardScaler())
])

# Categorical pipeline: fill missing values with the most frequent category, then one-hot encode
categorical_transformer = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
    ("encoder", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"))
])

# Combine both into one preprocessor
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(transformers=[
    ("num", numeric_transformer, numeric_cols),
    ("cat", categorical_transformer, categorical_cols)
])

print("Preprocessor built. It will be fit only on X_train when used inside a model Pipeline.")
```

```{python}
# Parts 2 & 3: D1- Baseline - DummyRegressor / DummyClassifier
# Regression & Classification baseline 

from sklearn.dummy import DummyRegressor, DummyClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import r2_score, mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error, accuracy_score

# --- Regression baseline ---
reg_baseline = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", DummyRegressor(strategy="mean"))
])
reg_baseline.fit(X_train, y_train_reg)
pred_reg = reg_baseline.predict(X_test)

print("=== Regression Baseline (Dummy) ===")
print("R²:", round(r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg), 4))
print("MAE:", round(mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg), 2))
print("RMSE:", round(mean_squared_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg) ** 0.5, 2))

# --- Classification baseline ---
clf_baseline = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", DummyClassifier(strategy="most_frequent"))
])
clf_baseline.fit(X_train, y_train_clf)
pred_clf = clf_baseline.predict(X_test)

print("\n=== Classification Baseline (Dummy) ===")
print("Accuracy:", round(accuracy_score(y_test_clf, pred_clf), 4))
# The floor every real model must beat. Regression R² ≈ 0 (predicting the average price every time explains none of the variance, by design).
# Classification accuracy ≈ 75% — simply matches the dataset's 75/25 class split by always guessing the majority class, without learning anything.
```

```{python}
#D2: Linear Model - Linear Regression/ Logistic Regression
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression, LogisticRegression

# --- Regression: Linear Regression (Sale's price)---
reg_linear = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", LinearRegression())
])
reg_linear.fit(X_train, y_train_reg)
pred_reg_lin = reg_linear.predict(X_test)

print("=== Regression: Linear Regression ===")
print("R²:", round(r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg_lin), 4))
print("MAE:", round(mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_lin), 2))
print("RMSE:", round(mean_squared_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_lin) ** 0.5, 2))

# --- Classification: Logistic Regression (Premium Home) ---
clf_logistic = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])
clf_logistic.fit(X_train, y_train_clf)
pred_clf_log = clf_logistic.predict(X_test)

print("\n=== Classification: Logistic Regression ===")
print("Accuracy:", round(accuracy_score(y_test_clf, pred_clf_log), 4))
```

```{python}
from sklearn.linear_model import Lasso

# Fit Lasso INSIDE a Pipeline, on training data only -- same leakage-safe pattern as before
lasso_selector = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", Lasso(alpha=100, random_state=RANDOM_STATE, max_iter=5000))
])
lasso_selector.fit(X_train, y_train_reg)

# Pull out the feature names after preprocessing (one-hot encoding creates many new column names)
feature_names = (
    numeric_cols +
    list(lasso_selector.named_steps["preprocessor"]
         .named_transformers_["cat"]
         .named_steps["encoder"]
         .get_feature_names_out(categorical_cols))
)

coefs = lasso_selector.named_steps["model"].coef_
nonzero = sum(coefs != 0)

print(f"Lasso kept {nonzero} out of {len(coefs)} features (rest shrunk to exactly 0)")

# Show the 15 most influential surviving features
coef_df = pd.DataFrame({"feature": feature_names, "coefficient": coefs})
coef_df = coef_df[coef_df["coefficient"] != 0].sort_values("coefficient", key=abs, ascending=False)
coef_df.head(15)
```

```{python}
#D3 - Decision Tree (and where it overfits)
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeRegressor, DecisionTreeClassifier

# --- Regression: Decision Tree ---
reg_tree = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", DecisionTreeRegressor(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])
reg_tree.fit(X_train, y_train_reg)
pred_reg_tree_train = reg_tree.predict(X_train)
pred_reg_tree_test = reg_tree.predict(X_test)

print("=== Regression: Decision Tree ===")
print("Train R²:", round(r2_score(y_train_reg, pred_reg_tree_train), 4))
print("Test R²:", round(r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg_tree_test), 4))
print("Test MAE:", round(mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_tree_test), 2))

# --- Classification: Decision Tree ---
clf_tree = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])
clf_tree.fit(X_train, y_train_clf)
pred_clf_tree_train = clf_tree.predict(X_train)
pred_clf_tree_test = clf_tree.predict(X_test)

print("\n=== Classification: Decision Tree ===")
print("Train Accuracy:", round(accuracy_score(y_train_clf, pred_clf_tree_train), 4))
print("Test Accuracy:", round(accuracy_score(y_test_clf, pred_clf_tree_test), 4))
```

```{python}
# Overfitting - 
#Where the Decision Tree overfits:
#Test R² peaks at max_depth≈5 (~0.865) and then gets worse as the tree is allowed to grow deeper, 
#while train R² keeps climbing all the way to a perfect 1.0. This means the tree starts overfitting beyond depth≈5 — past that point, it's no longer learning real price patterns, it's just memorizing individual training houses. The unrestricted tree (no depth limit) confirms this: train R²=1.0 vs. test R²=0.83 for regression, and train accuracy=1.0 vs. test accuracy=0.9249 for classification — both gaps are clear, measurable evidence of overfitting.

from sklearn.model_selection import validation_curve

# We sweep max_depth (the tree's complexity knob) and watch train vs test R²
# at each setting -- this shows us exactly where the tree starts memorizing
# training data instead of learning a generalizable pattern (overfitting).
depths = [2, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, None]
train_scores, test_scores = [], []

for d in depths:
    # Build a fresh tree at this depth, wrapped in our same leakage-safe Pipeline
    tree = Pipeline(steps=[
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        ("model", DecisionTreeRegressor(max_depth=d, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
    ])
    tree.fit(X_train, y_train_reg)

    # Score on training data (what the tree has seen) and test data (what it hasn't)
    train_scores.append(r2_score(y_train_reg, tree.predict(X_train)))
    test_scores.append(r2_score(y_test_reg, tree.predict(X_test)))

x_labels = [str(d) if d else "None" for d in depths]  # "None" = no depth limit at all

# Plot both curves together -- the GAP between them is the overfitting signal
plt.figure(figsize=(9,5))
plt.plot(x_labels, train_scores, "b-o", label="Train R²")
plt.plot(x_labels, test_scores, "r-o", label="Test R²")
plt.xlabel("max_depth")
plt.ylabel("R²")
plt.title("Decision Tree: Train vs Test R² by Depth (Overfitting Curve)")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

# Interpretation guide:
# - Shallow depth (e.g. 2-3): both scores lower -> underfitting, tree too simple to capture the pattern
# - Moderate depth: train and test scores stay close together -> good fit, generalizes well
# - Deep / unlimited depth: train R² approaches 1.0 while test R² drops or flattens -> overfitting,
#   the tree is memorizing individual training houses rather than learning real price relationships
print("Interpretation:")
print("- Shallow depth (e.g. 2-3): both scores lower (underfitting)")
print("- Moderate depth: train and test close together (good fit)")
print("- Deep / unlimited: train R² near 1.0, test R² drops or plateaus (overfitting)")
```

```{python}
#D4: XGBoost - with cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning
from xgboost import XGBRegressor, XGBClassifier

# --- Regression: XGBoost ---
reg_xgb = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", XGBRegressor(random_state=RANDOM_STATE, n_estimators=200, max_depth=4, learning_rate=0.1))
])
reg_xgb.fit(X_train, y_train_reg)
pred_reg_xgb_train = reg_xgb.predict(X_train)
pred_reg_xgb_test = reg_xgb.predict(X_test)

print("=== Regression: XGBoost ===")
print("Train R²:", round(r2_score(y_train_reg, pred_reg_xgb_train), 4))
print("Test R²:", round(r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg_xgb_test), 4))
print("Test MAE:", round(mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_xgb_test), 2))

# --- Classification: XGBoost ---
clf_xgb = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", XGBClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE, n_estimators=200, max_depth=4,
                             learning_rate=0.1, eval_metric="logloss"))
])
clf_xgb.fit(X_train, y_train_clf)
pred_clf_xgb_train = clf_xgb.predict(X_train)
pred_clf_xgb_test = clf_xgb.predict(X_test)

print("\n=== Classification: XGBoost ===")
print("Train Accuracy:", round(accuracy_score(y_train_clf, pred_clf_xgb_train), 4))
print("Test Accuracy:", round(accuracy_score(y_test_clf, pred_clf_xgb_test), 4))
```

```{python}
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# --- Regression: tune XGBoost with cross-validation ---
reg_xgb_pipeline = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", XGBRegressor(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])

param_grid_reg = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 200, 300],
    "model__max_depth": [3, 4, 5],
    "model__learning_rate": [0.05, 0.1]
}

reg_search = GridSearchCV(
    reg_xgb_pipeline, param_grid_reg, cv=5,
    scoring="r2", n_jobs=-1
)
reg_search.fit(X_train, y_train_reg)

print("=== Regression: XGBoost (tuned) ===")
print("Best params:", reg_search.best_params_)
print("Best CV R²:", round(reg_search.best_score_, 4))

best_reg_xgb = reg_search.best_estimator_
pred_reg_xgb_tuned = best_reg_xgb.predict(X_test)
print("Test R²:", round(r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg_xgb_tuned), 4))
print("Test MAE:", round(mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_xgb_tuned), 2))
```

```{python}
# --- Classification: tune XGBoost with cross-validation ---
clf_xgb_pipeline = Pipeline(steps=[
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", XGBClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE, eval_metric="logloss"))
])

param_grid_clf = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 200, 300],
    "model__max_depth": [3, 4, 5],
    "model__learning_rate": [0.05, 0.1]
}

clf_search = GridSearchCV(
    clf_xgb_pipeline, param_grid_clf, cv=5,
    scoring="accuracy", n_jobs=-1
)
clf_search.fit(X_train, y_train_clf)

print("=== Classification: XGBoost (tuned) ===")
print("Best params:", clf_search.best_params_)
print("Best CV Accuracy:", round(clf_search.best_score_, 4))

best_clf_xgb = clf_search.best_estimator_
pred_clf_xgb_tuned = best_clf_xgb.predict(X_test)
print("Test Accuracy:", round(accuracy_score(y_test_clf, pred_clf_xgb_tuned), 4))
```

```{python}
# PART 3 - Leaderboard table
import pandas as pd

leaderboard_reg = pd.DataFrame([
    {"Model": "Dummy (baseline)", "R2": r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg), "MAE": mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg), "RMSE": mean_squared_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg)**0.5},
    {"Model": "Linear Regression", "R2": r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg_lin), "MAE": mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_lin), "RMSE": mean_squared_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_lin)**0.5},
    {"Model": "Decision Tree", "R2": r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg_tree_test), "MAE": mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_tree_test), "RMSE": mean_squared_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_tree_test)**0.5},
    {"Model": "XGBoost (tuned)", "R2": r2_score(y_test_reg, pred_reg_xgb_tuned), "MAE": mean_absolute_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_xgb_tuned), "RMSE": mean_squared_error(y_test_reg, pred_reg_xgb_tuned)**0.5},
]).round(4)

print("=== Regression Leaderboard ===")
leaderboard_reg
```

```{python}

def clf_metrics(name, y_true, y_pred, y_proba):
    return {
        "Model": name,
        "Accuracy": accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred),
        "Precision": precision_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0),
        "Recall": recall_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0),
        "F1": f1_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0),
        "ROC-AUC": roc_auc_score(y_true, y_proba)
    }
    
```

```{python}
# Classification Leaderboad table 
from sklearn.metrics import precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score


def clf_metrics(name, y_true, y_pred, y_proba):
    return {
        "Model": name,
        "Accuracy": accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred),
        "Precision": precision_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0),
        "Recall": recall_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0),
        "F1": f1_score(y_true, y_pred, zero_division=0),
        "ROC-AUC": roc_auc_score(y_true, y_proba)
    }
leaderboard_clf = pd.DataFrame([
    clf_metrics("Dummy (baseline)", y_test_clf, pred_clf, clf_baseline.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]),
    clf_metrics("Logistic Regression", y_test_clf, pred_clf_log, clf_logistic.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]),
    clf_metrics("Decision Tree", y_test_clf, pred_clf_tree_test, clf_tree.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]),
    clf_metrics("XGBoost (tuned)", y_test_clf, pred_clf_xgb_tuned, best_clf_xgb.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]),
]).round(4)

print("=== Classification Leaderboard ===")
leaderboard_clf
```

```{python}
# Confusion matrix for our best classifier (XGBoost, tuned).
# Rows = actual class, columns = predicted class. The diagonal cells are
# correct predictions; the off-diagonal cells show exactly which mistakes
# the model makes (false positives vs false negatives), which a single
# accuracy number can't show us.
from sklearn.metrics import ConfusionMatrixDisplay

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6,5))
ConfusionMatrixDisplay.from_predictions(
    y_test_clf, pred_clf_xgb_tuned,
    display_labels=["Not Premium", "Premium"],
    cmap="Blues", ax=ax
)
ax.set_title("Confusion Matrix — XGBoost (tuned)")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Predicted vs Actual plot for our best regressor (XGBoost, tuned).
# Each point is one house: x = its real SalePrice, y = what the model predicted.
# The red dashed line is "perfect prediction" (predicted = actual exactly).
# Points clustered tightly around that line = accurate predictions;
# points far from the line = houses the model got noticeably wrong.
plt.figure(figsize=(7,7))
plt.scatter(y_test_reg, pred_reg_xgb_tuned, alpha=0.4)
lims = [y_test_reg.min(), y_test_reg.max()]
plt.plot(lims, lims, "r--", label="Perfect prediction")
plt.xlabel("Actual SalePrice")
plt.ylabel("Predicted SalePrice")
plt.title("Predicted vs Actual — XGBoost (tuned)")
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Confirm no missing values remain after the Pipeline's preprocessing step
import numpy as np
transformed = preprocessor.fit_transform(X_train)
print("Any missing values after preprocessing?", np.isnan(transformed.toarray()).any() if hasattr(transformed, "toarray") else np.isnan(transformed).any())
```

```{python}
import pickle

# Save the two final, tuned models. Each one's Pipeline already contains
# the fitted preprocessor as its first step (encoding + scaling + imputation),
# so loading reg_model.pkl / clf_model.pkl later also restores the
# preprocessing logic automatically -- no separate preprocessor.pkl needed.

with open("reg_model.pkl", "wb") as f:
    pickle.dump(best_reg_xgb, f)

with open("clf_model.pkl", "wb") as f:
    pickle.dump(best_clf_xgb, f)

print("Saved reg_model.pkl and clf_model.pkl")
```

```{python}
import pickle

with open("reg_model.pkl", "rb") as f:
    loaded = pickle.load(f)

print(loaded)  # shows the full Pipeline structure
print(loaded.named_steps)  # shows both steps: 'preprocessor' and 'model'
```
